---
title: "Week 5 — Reading the Frontier I + PAP Filing"
subtitle: "ASSIP 8-Week Empirical Finance Camp"
author: "Prof. Lei Gao, George Mason University"
date: "2026-07-20"
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD"
format:
  pptx:
    slide-level: 2
    incremental: false
    toc: false
    fig-width: 7.5
    fig-height: 4.2
    fig-dpi: 200
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
  freeze: auto
bibliography: ../bibliography.md
---

# Week 5 — Reading the Frontier I + PAP Filing {.title}

::: notes
Welcome to Week 5. For four weeks you have been a producer of empirical
work. This week we flip the verb. You become a consumer. The skill that
separates a student who has taken econometrics from one who can do
research is the ability to pick up a frontier paper and decide for
yourself whether to believe it. We train that muscle on five classics:
Fama and French nineteen ninety-two and ninety-three, Jegadeesh and
Titman, Petersen, and Bertrand, Duflo, and Mullainathan. And Friday,
you file a pre-analysis plan that freezes your capstone in writing.
:::

## Learning Objectives

- Apply the seven-part Reader's-Guide anatomy to any empirical paper
- Read tables first, prose last — spot the headline in 30 seconds
- Distinguish a cross-sectional study from a time-series factor model
- Diagnose when a paper's standard errors are honest
- File a Pre-Analysis Plan for your capstone by Friday, tagged PAP-v1

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Reading well and writing well are the same act run in opposite
directions.
:::

::: notes
Notice the verbs: apply, read, distinguish, diagnose, file. None of
them is memorize. By Friday you should be able to hand a stranger an
empirical paper and, in twenty minutes, tell them what it claims,
whether to believe it, and where it is soft. The PAP filing on Friday
closes the week. You will tag a git commit as PAP version one, which
freezes your capstone hypotheses, your specification, and your
falsification test before you have seen any results.
:::

## The Seven-Part Anatomy

Every paper this week gets the same seven boxes:

1. Research question — one sentence ending in a question mark
2. Identification strategy — design plus the identifying assumption
3. Data — source, sample, unit of observation, filters
4. Table-by-table reading order — which table is the headline
5. What's clever — the one genuinely good idea
6. What's vulnerable — the soft spot a referee will hit first
7. Three replication exercises — what you could build on a laptop

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
A sharp question without credible identification is a guess. Great
identification without data is a curiosity.
:::

::: notes
The order is not decoration. It is the order in which a claim has to
survive scrutiny. Box two is the most important — name the design and
then state the identifying assumption in one sentence. Box four teaches
you to find the headline number off the table itself before reading a
single word of the authors' framing. And box seven turns reading into
doing — three things a student could replicate on a laptop. Apply
these boxes to Fama and French on Monday, and to an unseen paper on
Friday's assessment.
:::

## Monday — Fama & French 1992: Beta Dies

Research question: what explains why some stocks earn higher average returns than others?

- CAPM's verdict: only market beta should matter
- The data's verdict: size and book-to-market matter; beta is flat
- Method: portfolio sorts plus Fama–MacBeth cross-sectional regression
- Sample: nonfinancial CRSP–Compustat stocks, 1963 to 1990

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
The dog that didn't bark. Beta is the number that should be big under
CAPM and isn't — that absence is the finding.
:::

::: notes
Sam walked into Lab 2 with the working belief that riskier stocks pay
more. Monday's paper is the autopsy on that belief. The move you watch
is the double sort. Sort stocks into size-and-beta portfolios, then
slide across beta within a row of roughly constant size, and watch
whether average return climbs. CAPM says it must. What you see is close
to flat. Then put beta against size and book-to-market in one
regression. Beta's slope collapses; the other two survive. The paper
did not add a variable. It dethroned one.
:::

## Tuesday — Fama & French 1993: The Three-Factor Machine

FF92 said size and book-to-market are *priced*. FF93 turns them into *factors* on the right-hand side.

$$
R_{it} - R_{ft} = \alpha_i + b_i\,(R_{Mt}-R_{ft}) + s_i\,\text{SMB}_t + h_i\,\text{HML}_t + \varepsilon_{it}
$$

- SMB = small minus big; HML = high minus low book-to-market
- Test assets: the 25 size–BE/ME portfolios
- Pricing test: GRS joint $F$-statistic on all 25 intercepts

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Cross-section asks "what is priced?" Time series asks "do these
factors explain co-movement and leave no alpha?"
:::

::: notes
Students conflate these two papers constantly. FF92 is cross-sectional —
freeze a month, regress returns on characteristics. FF93 is time series
— freeze a portfolio, regress its monthly returns on factor returns.
The factors are real long-short portfolios: SMB is small minus big,
HML is high minus low book-to-market. The regression hands you two
things the cross-section cannot: an R-squared, which says the factors
capture common variation, and an alpha, the pricing error. The GRS
test asks whether all twenty-five intercepts are jointly zero.
:::

## Wednesday — Jegadeesh & Titman 1993: Momentum

Buy past winners, short past losers, hold 3 to 12 months. It works.

- Sort stocks into deciles on past $J$-month return
- Hold the winner-minus-loser spread for $K$ months
- Classic 6/6: roughly one percent per month, survives risk adjustment
- Overlapping cohorts inflate the t-stat — Newey–West with lag $K-1$ is mandatory

::: callout-warning
## Watch out
A profitable rule built only from past returns strikes weak-form
efficiency. Either a risk factor is missing, or prices do not impound
information cleanly.
:::

::: notes
Sam has been waiting all week for this one. Jegadeesh and Titman take a
strategy a sixteen-year-old day-trader would invent on a napkin and
show it earns about one percent per month at the classic six-month,
six-month window. The methodological subtlety students miss is the
overlapping-holding-period trick. You form a new winner-minus-loser
portfolio every month and hold each cohort for K months. That uses
every month of data, but it induces serial correlation. The Week 2
fix — Newey–West HAC with lag at least K minus one — is mandatory.
:::

## Thursday — Petersen 2009: Which Standard Error Is Honest?

Petersen surveys finance journals and finds chaos. Same regression, six methods, t-stats from 5 to 1.8.

- Monte Carlo identification: build a world where you *know* the truth
- Firm effect → cluster by firm; time effect → cluster by time
- White / heteroskedasticity-robust does *not* fix within-cluster correlation
- Fama–MacBeth corrects a time effect but misses a firm effect

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
"Robust" does not mean "robust to everything." Match the cluster to
the correlation your residuals actually have.
:::

::: notes
A methods paper claims something about how we should do empirical work.
You cannot check it against a stylized fact. The only way is to find a
world where you already know the right answer, and watch which method
recovers it. Petersen's cleverest move: he identifies the truth by
building it himself, in Monte Carlo simulations where he sets the
true beta and the true error structure. The lesson Priya and Maya
carry forward is the taxonomy. White-robust is barely better than
classical OLS when a firm effect is present.
:::

## Friday — Bertrand, Duflo & Mullainathan 2004: The Placebo Law

The question: do conventional DiD standard errors overstate significance?

- Real CPS state-year panel; assign a *fake* law to random states
- True effect is zero by construction; rejections are false positives
- Headline: conventional t-stats reject around 40 percent of the time, not 5
- Fix: cluster by state, block bootstrap, or collapse to pre/post

::: callout-warning
## Watch out
The disease is serial correlation in slow-moving outcomes. The longer
the panel, the worse the over-rejection.
:::

::: notes
The placebo-law experiment is one of the cleanest identification moves
in empirical economics. Take a real state-year panel with all its
serial correlation baked in. Invent a fake law. Pick random states and
a random year and declare them treated. Nothing actually happened, so
the true effect is exactly zero. Run the regression thousands of times.
If standard errors were honest you would reject five percent of the
time. The actual rejection rate is roughly forty percent. Priya saw
this last week when her wildfire t-stat fell from five to one point eight.
:::

## Mentor Session: Anatomy of a JF Paper

Prof. Gao dissects his own working paper (*Rainbow of Credits*, AEA 2025) from the *inside*.

- A sharp question with a one-sentence counterfactual
- A credible research design with the identifying assumption stated explicitly
- Transparent tables: key coefficient marches across columns as controls pile on
- Honest robustness: the months spent trying to kill your own result

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
The anatomy you apply outward to strangers' papers is the anatomy you
apply inward when you design your own.
:::

::: notes
Wednesday's mentor session flips the camera. All week you have been a
reader. Now I show you what it looks like to be a writer. I take one
of my own working papers and walk you through the two years of false
starts that produced it. The published paper hides those false starts
beautifully. Today I expose them. Bring your written warm-ups — we
will work through Maya, Devon, Priya, Sam, and Leah's capstone
questions together.
:::

## Common Pitfalls When Reading Papers

1. Reading front-to-back instead of tables-first — you get sold by the intro
2. Confusing FF92 (cross-section) with FF93 (time-series factor model)
3. Quoting a t-stat from an overlapping-portfolio backtest without Newey–West
4. Believing "White-robust" is robust to clustered errors
5. Treating placebo failure as a curiosity rather than a verdict on the method

::: callout-warning
## Watch out
Each of these will make a paper look stronger than it is. Reading the
table before the prose is the best inoculation against an author's framing.
:::

::: notes
Each pitfall has been published, repeatedly, by people who should know
better. The FF92 versus FF93 confusion is rampant because students
remember "size and value" but not the structural difference between a
cross-sectional regression and a time-series factor model. White-robust
is the single most over-trusted standard error in finance, and
Petersen's whole paper is the case against it under correlated errors.
:::

## Friday Deliverable — File Your PAP

Friday afternoon you tag a git commit `PAP-v1`. The PAP freezes:

- One-sentence research question with the implied counterfactual
- Primary specification: outcome, key regressor, controls, fixed effects, clustering
- Sample window, filters, and data sources
- The falsification test: what result would make you abandon the hypothesis?

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
From Friday on, what counts as a result is fixed in writing. A
specification you wrote down before seeing the data has standing; one
you wrote down after does not.
:::

::: notes
Friday is the day your capstone becomes real. Every student files a
Pre-Analysis Plan, tagged in your project repo as PAP version one.
Maya, your fair-lending question. Devon, crypto adoption. Priya,
climate-insurance pricing. Sam, intraday momentum. Leah, R-and-D and
innovation. By Friday at five, each of you has a tagged commit. The
distinction between a confirmatory test, declared in PAP-v1, and an
exploratory analysis declared after the fact, is the cleanest honesty
technology our field has.
:::

## What's Next — Week 6

- Reading-the-frontier continues: governance, common ownership, fintech credit
- Capstone work begins in earnest against your frozen PAP
- Wednesday mentor: matching your design to the right standard error
- Friday: first-look regressions — and the discipline of not re-writing the PAP

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
The PAP is a promise to your future self. Next week you start cashing
it in against the data.
:::

::: notes
Week 6 is when you run the regressions your PAP committed you to. Any
deviation from the PAP gets documented in writing, with a reason. The
reading skills you built this week are now turned against your own
work — applying the seven-part anatomy inward, exactly as the mentor
session promised.
:::

## References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::

::: notes
The references slide is auto-populated by Pandoc from cited keys
including Fama and French nineteen ninety-two and ninety-three,
Jegadeesh and Titman, Petersen two thousand nine, and Bertrand, Duflo,
and Mullainathan two thousand four.
:::
